# Comparative Analysis: Capability‑Native Governance vs. Microsoft Agent Governance Toolkit (AGT)

**Executive Summary:** Microsoft’s **Agent Governance Toolkit (AGT)** and the **Capability‑Native Governance** model share the goal of enforcing **fine-grained, runtime security** for autonomous AI agents, but they differ in design philosophy, enforcement mechanisms, and integration approach. **AGT** provides a comprehensive, in-process **policy enforcement layer** (a “policy kernel”) that intercepts every agent action and evaluates it against dynamic rules with *sub-millisecond latency*. It is an **open-source, multi-language middleware** that integrates with popular agent frameworks to provide *address the OWASP Top 10*\* agent risks (e.g. tool misuse, goal hijacking) through a combination of deterministic policy checks, cryptographic agent identity, trust scoring, SRE-style reliability controls, and compliance logging. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle) [\[opensource...rosoft.com\]](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/02/introducing-the-agent-governance-toolkit-open-source-runtime-security-for-ai-agents/), [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)

By contrast, the **Capability-Native Governance** approach emphasizes *object-capability security principles* and **mechanical enforcement** of least privilege. Agents are initially launched with **zero ambient privileges** (no default network or file system access), and all actions require possession of an **explicit, unforgeable token (capability)** that grants *just enough authority* for that specific operation. A **secure gateway** intercepts tool/API calls and deterministically checks for a valid capability token authorizing *that exact action on that exact resource* before allowing execution. This decouples trust from the agent’s own reasoning: *the agent can propose any action, but it has no power to execute unless a separate authorization service (or pre-issued credential) says “yes”*. The focus is on **minimizing the system’s reliance on interpreting agent intent** and maximizing the use of **externally verifiable, least-privilege credentials** (like capability tokens or verifiable credentials) to constrain agent actions. [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents)

**In summary**, AGT offers a broad, ready-to-use solution that integrates easily with existing platforms and addresses a wide spectrum of governance concerns (security, reliability, compliance) with minimal performance overhead. The capability-native model drills deeper on core security invariants – eliminating ambient authority and ensuring every action is pre-authorized – potentially providing stronger isolation and vendor-neutral, standardized enforcement at the cost of requiring more architectural changes and currently lacking off-the-shelf tooling. Below we provide a detailed side-by-side analysis: [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)

<!-- Copilot-Researcher-Visualization -->

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

[\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle), [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents)

## Architectural and Enforcement Model

Both approaches enforce a **runtime policy** on agent actions, but they differ in how and where that policy is defined and enforced, as summarized below:

| **Aspect**                   | **Capability‑Native Governance** (Proposal)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | **Microsoft AGT** (Agent Governance Toolkit)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Core Authorization Model** | **Object-capability tokens**: Every action requires a cryptographically signed *capability token* that explicitly authorizes that specific operation (including action type, target resource, and constraints) [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents). Authorization is granular and bounded to *one operation*, with **no ambient or default privileges** beyond what tokens confer.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | **Centralized policy rules**: Every action is intercepted by a **Policy Engine** (“Agent OS”) that checks if the action is allowed by a set of **declarative policies** (in YAML, Rego, or Cedar) loaded in-memory [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). Agents can have default capabilities (tools or actions) enabled or disabled via policy; any action not explicitly allowed by policy is denied by default [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| **Policy Evaluation**        | **External, token-based**: Authorization logic is applied *outside* the agent via token issuance and validation. A **Capability Issuer** (potentially separate service) grants tokens for allowed actions based on pre-defined policies or on-the-fly validation. The agent must present the correct token to a secure gateway at execution time [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents). Policy is encoded in the tokens themselves (as constraints on action/resource) or in the issuance logic, and **enforcement is purely mechanical** by token verification (no natural language or ML needed) [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents). | **In-process, rule-based**: A **Policy Enforcement Point** (PEP) inside the agent’s process intercepts each action invocation and delegates to a **Policy Decision Point** (PDP) which evaluates the action against loaded rules [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). Policies can incorporate conditions on the agent’s context (e.g., allow writing file only if path matches pattern) and can even include an LLM-based classifier for intent (to mitigate goal hijacking) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). This allows expressive policies but relies on the correctness of the policy definitions and any ML classifiers.                                                                                                                                                 |
| **Enforcement Point**        | **Out-of-process secure gateway**: Typically implemented as a sidecar service or proxy (e.g., API gateway) at the boundary of the agent’s execution environment. It requires that the agent present a valid capability token for each action; otherwise, the action is blocked [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents). This gateway runs *outside* the agent’s process (e.g., as a separate container or process) and thus forms a strong isolation layer (the agent cannot bypass it to reach tools or external resources due to network/file system restrictions) [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents).                                 | **In-process middleware**: Integrated via SDK or hooks in the agent’s runtime environment. The PEP is typically a library (e.g., Python middleware, or a plugin in the agent’s code) that intercepts calls via monkey patching or framework callbacks [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). It operates within the agent’s process space. This is convenient and high-performance, but it means the enforcement is at the application layer. The AGT README explicitly notes it’s not a separate OS-level sandbox: *the policy engine and the agents run in the same process* [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). A compromised agent process, in theory, could try to bypass or tamper with the in-process controls (just as any code can potentially bypass a library).          |
| **Default Privileges**       | **Zero by default**: Agents start with **no ambient permissions** – e.g., no default network access, no file or database access, no system calls beyond what the sandbox permits [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents). All privileges must be explicitly granted via tokens. If an agent tries to perform an operation without a token, it fails by design (e.g., network calls blocked by firewall, file opens fail due to lack of OS file handles, API calls rejected by missing auth) [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents). This ensures a *deny-by-default* posture at a fundamental level.                                         | **Configurable allow/deny lists**: By default, AGT can be configured to deny all tools and actions except those explicitly allowed by policy [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). However, the agent’s process still *has the ability to attempt* actions; the toolkit just intercepts and stops disallowed ones. Without additional OS-level isolation, the agent process still runs with the environment’s privileges (e.g., it might have general network connectivity or file system access) but the toolkit’s hooks intercept common libraries (HTTP calls, file I/O, etc.) to enforce policy. In practice, teams are advised to pair AGT with standard infrastructure controls (containers, network policies) for high-risk deployments [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). |
| **Isolation & TCB**          | **Stronger (OS-level)**: The design assumes a **strong isolation boundary** between agent and enforcement. The agent runs in a locked-down environment (e.g., container or sandbox VM) with no ability to bypass the secure gateway. The trusted computing base (TCB) includes the gateway, the token issuer, and the minimal OS/hypervisor enforcing sandboxing. This reduces risk that a compromised agent can subvert the guardrails. The trade-off is requiring a more complex deployment (multiple processes/containers and careful OS configuration) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle).                                                                                                                             | **Software middleware (same process)**: AGT’s TCB is the agent’s host process and its loaded governance libraries. The toolkit significantly hardens agent behavior *within* that process but does not in itself create a separate OS-level security boundary [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). For full isolation, Microsoft recommends still using containers, network ACLs, and IAM in conjunction [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). Thus, AGT by itself is an *application-layer control* – a compromised agent process (if it exploited a vulnerability in the runtime or AGT libraries) could potentially circumvent these controls. However, the in-process design makes adoption simpler since it slots into an agent’s existing runtime without new infrastructure. |

**Interpretation:** The capability-native approach treats **authorization like OS-level capabilities** – tying specific privileges to unforgeable tokens and enforcing them in a separate, minimal runtime environment. This provides a *mechanical, deterministic* check (presence or absence of a token) and a true deny-by-default stance at the system level. In contrast, AGT’s approach is to **intercept and evaluate** – it acts as a **policy kernel** within the agent’s process that can be extremely fast and flexible but still shares the execution environment with the agent code. [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)

**Enforcement & Policy:** AGT’s PEP/PDP model allows rich policy conditions (time-based rules, user-defined allow/deny lists, even content-based checks using an LLM for high-level intent) whereas the pure capability model focuses on *pre-defining exactly what is allowed in the token* and then performing a binary check. The trade-off: **AGT offers flexibility and easier integration** with existing security frameworks (e.g., one can express complex rules without re-launching agents or issuing new tokens), but **policy complexity can introduce risk** (policy misconfigurations or relying on LLM-based checks have failure modes). The capability approach’s simplicity yields a smaller attack surface – there’s no risk of mis-parsing a policy or a malicious prompt fooling the enforcement layer, because the enforcement doesn’t interpret intent or context, it only sees tokens and static attributes. However, that also means it cannot make nuanced decisions beyond the scope of the token’s encoded parameters without issuing new tokens. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle) [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents)

**Integration and Ecosystem:** AGT’s architecture as a language-agnostic library (with Python, TS, .NET, Go, Rust support) directly targets ease-of-use – it’s a pip/nuget/npm install with many adapters, so hooking it into an existing agent is straightforward (most frameworks require adding a few lines of configuration or middleware). The capability approach, in 2026, is more conceptual – early implementations exist (e.g., open-source projects like *Tenuo* and *Crittora’s Agent Permission Protocol*) demonstrating capability-token ideas, but there isn’t yet a single, unified toolkit. Adopting a pure capability model today likely means more custom engineering: building a service to issue and verify tokens, modifying agents to request and handle tokens preemptively, and orchestrating secure external proxies for all tools. In contrast, AGT provides an integrated solution out-of-the-box, which is especially attractive if you’re already in the Microsoft/Azure ecosystem or using common agent frameworks. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle) [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents)

## Identity and Delegation Mechanisms

Both systems recognize that **agent identity** and **delegation of authority** are crucial problems, but they address them differently:

| **Aspect**           | **Capability-Native Governance**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | **Microsoft AGT**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Agent Identity**   | **Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs)**: Agents are provisioned with unique cryptographic identities (e.g., `did:web:enterprise.com:agent:12345` or `did:ion:...`) and associated key pairs [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). These identities are self-contained and verifiable via public key infrastructure (DNS, blockchain, etc.), allowing agent actions to be signed and verified anywhere without a central identity provider. The enterprise’s IAM is used to bootstrap trust (e.g., tying an agent’s DID to a user or service at issuance time), but after issuance the agent operates under its own identity. This **enables cross-organization trust** via standard credential exchange (W3C Verifiable Credentials), as any external service can validate an agent’s DID and token signature without direct integration to the issuer’s IAM [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/), [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents). | **Enterprise Identity with optional DID bridging**: By default, AGT assigns each agent a DID (`did:agentmesh:...`) and Ed25519 key for signing actions and credentials [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle), thereby giving each agent a cryptographic identity. In enterprise environments, AGT can integrate with **Entra Agent ID** (Azure AD’s native agent identity feature) via an identity bridge [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). In that mode, each agent is represented as an Azure AD object (with an **Object ID** and **associated human sponsor**), and AGT maps the agent’s DID to that directory identity [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). This combined approach yields two layers: AGT’s DID-based identity for runtime (used in the “Inter-Agent Trust Protocol” for agent-to-agent auth and message signing) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle), and an Azure AD identity for enterprise policy (Conditional Access, sponsor-based approvals, etc.) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). Without bridging, AGT’s DIDs function in a closed system, requiring separate integration for external trust. With Entra Agent ID integration, agent identities can be managed like users in Azure AD (appearing in **Agent365** dashboards, subject to Access Reviews and Conditional Access) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). |
| **Delegation Model** | **Explicit capability delegation/attenuation**: Agents can only delegate authority by **passing a subset of their own capabilities** to another agent (or sub-process). The system ensures the “no new privileges” rule: a child agent’s tokens must be derived from a parent’s tokens and can only narrow, never expand, the scope [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). For example, an agent with “read/write” on Database X can delegate a “read-only” token for a specific table to a helper agent – but it cannot grant access to a different database or a longer duration than it has. This is enforced by the Capability Issuer or Attenuator service at delegation time, typically using cryptographic linking of child tokens to parent tokens (so the chain of delegation is provable during audit) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). Depth of delegation can also be limited. This ensures a compromised sub-agent cannot gain more power than its progenitor.                                         | **Hierarchical agent identities with sponsor tracking**: AGT’s Agent Mesh supports *scoped child identities* and delegation via the `delegate()` function [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). Under the hood, this creates a new DID and key pair for the sub-agent and automatically attenuates the child’s policy or trust score relative to its parent. The toolkit enforces *monotonic privileges*: children can be restricted to a subset of tools or actions. However, because policy is rule-based, this attenuation must be codified in the rules (e.g., parent agent dynamically loads a policy for the child or passes along context that the PDP uses to limit the child). The delegation is more at the identity level – the parent agent can spawn a new agent with a linked identity and limited trust. The bridging with Entra ID ensures that the **human sponsor context flows down** – a chain of custody is maintained such that actions by sub-agents are still attributed to the original user’s identity in Azure AD logs [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). While AGT supports delegation, implementers must design the specific policy/permission passing; it’s not an automatically generated token chain in the same way as the capability model’s cryptographic attenuation, but rather a combination of AGT’s internal **sponsor binding** and any whitelisting rules for child agents.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |

**Key differences in identity & delegation:** The **capability model takes an object-security view** – an agent’s authority is literally embodied in tokens it holds, which can be **selectively given to others** (conceptually similar to handing someone a key that only opens certain doors). AGT, on the other hand, **starts with identity** – giving each agent a verifiable identity (DID or Azure AD account) and then applying policies to that identity. Delegation in AGT means creating a new identity tied to the original (with possibly an inheritance of some permissions or a policy context), rather than cryptographically minting a new subset credential. *Both approaches can achieve least-privilege delegation*, but the capability model ensures it at the token level (child tokens are mathematically constrained), while AGT ensures it at the policy level (child agents are issued separate identities and policies that must be crafted to be subsets). AGT’s approach may offer more flexibility in complex multi-agent scenarios (e.g., you can set **behavioral rules** for sub-agents, such as different trust score thresholds or SLOs), whereas the capability model offers a simple and robust guarantee that children can *never* exceed their parent’s authority by construction. [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)

**Cross-domain portability:** Because capability tokens are based on open standards (JWTs, *Verifiable Credentials*) and tied to DIDs, the capability-native model is inherently suited for cross-organization scenarios – any service that trusts the issuer’s DID or public key can verify the token, no matter where the agent is running. AGT is also multi-cloud and open-source, but certain features (like Entra Agent ID integration or Purview scanning) are specific to Microsoft’s ecosystem. AGT’s use of DIDs means agents from different organizations *could* establish mutual trust via DIDs, but that currently requires coordination (e.g., exchanging or recognizing DIDs). The **Inter-Agent Trust Protocol (IATP)** in AGT’s Agent Mesh is a step toward a **standard for agent identity and communication** across domains, but it’s primarily designed for agents all using AGT. In contrast, the capability model aligns with broader decentralized identity efforts (like W3C DID and Verifiable Credentials standards) for maximum interoperability beyond a single vendor, at the cost of needing those standards to be adopted across the industry (which is in progress but not yet universal). [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)

## Security and Threat Modeling

Both AGT and the capability-native approach aim for **zero-trust principles** and address similar threat scenarios (as outlined by the OWASP Agentic AI Top 10), but their methods differ. Here’s how they compare on key risk areas: [\[opensource...rosoft.com\]](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/02/introducing-the-agent-governance-toolkit-open-source-runtime-security-for-ai-agents/), [\[digitalapplied.com\]](https://www.digitalapplied.com/blog/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-runtime-security)

*   **Prompt Injection / Goal Hijacking:**
    *   **AGT:** Introduces a *semantic intent classifier* in the policy engine to detect malicious or off-policy instructions before they lead to action. This classifier (an LLM or heuristic) tries to catch suspicious agent intents (e.g., instructions to perform disallowed actions) and can block or flag them. This reduces risk but is not foolproof – as with any AI-based filter, there's room for adversarial bypass (the “guard model” could be tricked by clever phrasing, similar to how content filters are evaded). The toolkit addresses every OWASP category but *“governs actions, not free-form reasoning,”* as Venkat Peri notes, meaning once an instruction becomes an action it can be blocked, but the internal thought process of the agent isn’t fully controllable. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)
    *   **Capability Model:** Avoids trying to interpret or intervene in the agent’s cognition. Instead, it focuses on *limiting consequences* – a prompt injection might convince an agent to attempt something nefarious, but if that action isn’t pre-authorized by a token, it will simply hit a wall and be denied with no effect. The upside is a very reliable defense: no prompt injection can create a token out of thin air. The downside is that an agent could still produce harmful content or misuse an allowed action (e.g., sending an offensive email to an authorized recipient) because the system doesn’t analyze intent – it only cares whether the action is permitted. Addressing that might require separate layers (monitoring outputs, adding human-in-the-loop steps for sensitive actions, etc.), which parallels AGT’s own recommendation to incorporate human review for high-impact decisions (something not solved by AGT either, as the “honest take” points out HITL workflows are outside its current scope). [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)

*   **Tool Misuse & Privilege Escalation:**
    *   **AGT:** Provides *policy-defined allow/deny lists for tools and actions* and monitors usage frequency and patterns. The Agent OS policy can restrict an agent from calling dangerous tools or using certain APIs altogether, and the Agent Runtime can impose limits like execution rings (preventing an agent from performing privileged operations in certain contexts). If an agent somehow tries to step out of bounds, it’s denied by the policy PEP. However, as noted, since AGT is in-process, a sufficiently advanced attacker with code execution might attempt to bypass these checks, which is why additional isolation is recommended for hardened scenarios. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)
    *   **Capability Model:** Ensures *by design* that tool misuse leading to privilege escalation is impossible as long as the token system isn’t itself compromised. An agent that wasn’t handed a certain capability *cannot call those tools or access those resources* – the calls will fail at the gateway or via missing OS privileges. This provides strong **confused deputy protection**: an agent tricked into misusing a privileged interface will simply lack the token to do so, and the request will be blocked. Furthermore, by using a **monotonic delegation** scheme, the model guarantees that if an agent spawns sub-agents (deputies), they cannot accumulate more privileges than the original (children’s tokens are strictly subset of parent’s). AGT also aims for this (it provides `delegate()` for scoped child identities), but because AGT’s policies are not themselves tokenized, it’s theoretically possible (though unlikely) that a misconfiguration could allow a child to have a broader policy than the parent. In practice, both approaches, if used correctly, prevent privilege escalation; the capability model’s *guarantee is mathematical (child tokens derived from parent tokens) while AGT’s guarantee is through proper configuration of policy inheritance or context logic*. [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)

*   **Rogue or Compromised Agent Containment:**
    *   **AGT:** Implements *multiple layers of failsafes*, including a **kill switch** that can instantly terminate an agent’s actions across the system (by killing its process or halting its threads) and drop its trust score to minimum. It also has **behavioral monitoring** via the trust score (Agent Mesh’s 0–1000 trust rating). A malicious or erratic agent will see its score decay and can be automatically moved to a “suspended” state or have elevated actions blocked when below a threshold. Additionally, Agent SRE’s circuit breakers can cut off an agent that’s misbehaving (e.g., making too many rapid requests or causing errors) to prevent system overload. Logging and integration with Sentinel/Defender means alerts can be generated for security to intervene quickly, e.g., disabling an Agent ID in Azure AD which instantly prevents further auth token issuance. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)
    *   **Capability Model:** Also supports an *immediate kill-switch*, typically by blacklisting an agent’s identity or revoking its active tokens, so that any further actions are denied in real time. Because tokens are short-lived (minutes) by default, even without a manual kill most of an agent’s credentials will expire soon, limiting how long a rogue agent can act. The model’s simplicity aids detection – a spike in denied actions in the logs (because an agent is attempting operations it has no tokens for) is an obvious red flag, which can trigger automated containment (we envisioned pairing the gateway logs with SIEM rules to catch repeated policy violations quickly). However, the capability framework itself doesn’t assign “trust scores” or throttle an agent’s allowed actions based on behavior; it’s a binary allow/deny. In practice, you would complement it with monitoring systems (just as we described hooking Gateway denies to an alerting system). In short, AGT bakes in some *autonomous response* (e.g., trust decay and automatic kill on critical policy violation), whereas the capability model would rely on external monitoring or simple counters (e.g., usage limits in tokens, which AGT also supports to some extent) to detect and stop abnormal behavior. Both approaches can achieve quick revocation (AGT by disabling an identity or flipping a kill-switch flag in memory, capability model by pushing a revocation entry that makes all further token checks fail), typically within seconds, especially since each new action in either system results in a fresh check where revocation can be applied. [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)

*   **Replay and Token Misuse:**
    *   **AGT:** Uses **short-lived credentials and optional DPoP**-like proofs for inter-agent communication to prevent replay. Each agent-to-agent message can be signed with the sender’s DID key, and tokens (like Agent Mesh’s internal capability tokens or credentials) expire quickly (15 minutes by default). However, AGT’s primary model is PEP/PDP, so the typical scenario is not handing tokens to call internal tools but rather directly evaluating and executing or denying the call. Replay in that context is less about external tokens (except for agent-to-agent calls) and more about, say, an agent repeatedly attempting a denied action – which the policy engine will consistently block. The trust score can also be used to identify repeated attempts and low confidence (for example, trust could be lowered after each denied action). [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)
    *   **Capability Model:** Each token is essentially a *one-time or limited-use capability* – once it’s used (or after a short time window) it becomes invalid. This one-time nature makes replay difficult: even if an attacker exfiltrated a token, it would either have already been used or would expire shortly, minimizing the window for reuse. To further harden against token theft, a proof-of-possession mechanism can be implemented (e.g., binding tokens to the agent’s private key or secure context) similar to DPoP, ensuring that stolen tokens cannot be successfully used by others. Overall, both systems prioritize **short token lifetimes and binding**; AGT’s emphasis is on automatic rotation and revoking tokens via trust decisions, while the capability model tends to rely on expiry and explicit revocation when necessary. [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents)

*   **Parallel or Multi-Agent Scenarios:** Both models allow multiple agents to operate under governance, but with slight differences:
    *   **AGT:** Provides a **global view** of agents with features like **Agent Mesh’s trust scoring and the Agent SRE’s telemetry**, which can be aggregated for all agents. AGT can manage a cluster of agents, each with their own identity, and enforce both per-agent and system-wide policies (for example, limiting the total number of certain actions across all agents to prevent system overload). Because AGT can integrate with enterprise identity (Entra Agent ID), it can tie all agents to known organizational entities and apply company-wide access policies and monitoring through existing tools like Azure AD Conditional Access and Microsoft Sentinel. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)
    *   **Capability Model:** Focuses on the **individual agent’s envelope of authority** rather than a holistic overview. It doesn’t inherently provide an aggregated trust score or system-level throttle (though one could build these on top by analyzing the capability logs). Each agent operates in its own sandbox with its allocated capabilities, and interactions between agents are controlled through capability passing. A multi-agent orchestration requires carefully designing which tokens the main orchestrator shares with sub-agents. The advantage is that if one agent is compromised, its peers remain unaffected unless it had been given tokens to impact them, thus naturally limiting cascade effects. The disadvantage is that the system doesn’t automatically “know” relationships or track global state like trust levels across agents — such intelligence would have to be built on the outside (e.g., a manager service that monitors all the secure gateways and issues adaptive tokens or cutoffs).

In summary, **both systems aim for a *deny-by-default, least privilege* environment**, but they execute it differently. **AGT uses a dynamic policy engine** inside the agent app to catch or stop forbidden actions just in time, whereas **the capability model uses externalized, signed authorizations and OS-level controls** to prevent the agent from ever executing forbidden actions in the first place. AGT’s approach is easier to integrate and provides a holistic suite of security features (policy, identity, reliability, compliance) out-of-the-box, while the capability model’s approach can offer stronger guarantees on containment and cross-platform compatibility if implemented thoroughly, at the cost of additional development and potentially less feature richness in areas like monitoring and trust metrics.

## Developer Experience and Operational Considerations

**Ease of Adoption:**

*   **Microsoft AGT** is designed for quick adoption by development teams. Its **framework-agnostic adapters** for popular agent libraries (LangChain, Semantic Kernel, etc.) mean that adding AGT is usually a matter of a few lines of configuration or code registration. For example, in LangChain you might add a callback handler to route all tool calls through AGT’s `AgentOS` PEP; in a custom agent, you wrap your tool invocation functions with AGT’s policy check. Because it’s a library, it can typically be introduced without significant changes to how the agent is deployed (no new services to run; it lives in-process). Microsoft’s multi-language support (Python, TS/JS, .NET, Go, Rust) ensures that whether your agent is a Python script or a Node.js app, there is a compatible AGT package. The trade-off is that **teams must invest in writing and maintaining the policy rules**. AGT doesn’t magically know what tools or actions to permit; you need to define YAML policies or code (Rego, Cedar rules) specific to your business needs. Microsoft provides samples (like a default policy that might forbid certain dangerous operations), but these are not production-ready. Thus, adopting AGT means not only installing the packages but also doing the cross-functional work of defining policies for your domain and deciding what constitutes safe vs unsafe agent behavior. This is a common challenge in using any policy engine: it requires a governance strategy beyond just technology. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)
*   **Capability-Native Model** demands a **bigger initial engineering effort** if starting from scratch, because it’s an architectural paradigm, not a drop-in library (yet). Developers would need to set up a capability issuance service (or integrate with an existing one, if using something like Crittora’s Agent Permission Protocol or similar emerging standards), and modify their agent code to obtain and attach tokens to each action or tool call. They also need to deploy and manage the secure gateway/proxy for tool calls (which could be an API Gateway or sidecar microservice). In exchange for this upfront complexity, the model can simplify policy management in the long run: **capability policies are naturally fine-grained** (the token either allows the action or not, possibly with some constraints). The “policy” becomes the logic in the capability issuer (which could be as simple as “if user X is allowed Y, then give agent token for Y”), plus the design of capability manifests. Many of the tricky parts of policy design (like multiple conditions or parsing natural language) are avoided – you don’t write an allow/deny rule in code; you structure what tokens to issue. One can even imagine auto-generating required tokens by analyzing an agent’s code or by running the agent in a safe environment to see what it tries to do (and then granting only those tokens in production). Still, as of now, the capability approach requires assembling pieces (auth service, tokenization of tools, etc.) largely manually, whereas AGT is a **ready-made toolkit with documentation and tutorials** (20+ step-by-step tutorials, per the Microsoft blog, and a community forum). In short, AGT currently has the edge in developer convenience, but this could change as open standards and tools for capability-based agent security mature (there are already early libraries like `tenuo` and `crittora` proposing similar patterns). [\[opensource...rosoft.com\]](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/02/introducing-the-agent-governance-toolkit-open-source-runtime-security-for-ai-agents/), [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)

**Policy vs. Capabilities – Admin and Dev Effort:**
A key distinction is how one expresses the “rules” for the agent:

*   *In AGT*, rules can be high-level (e.g., *“Allow read access to database records that pass a PII filter”* or *“Deny sending emails outside @mycompany.com domain”*). These rules can consider attributes of the action or in some cases its content (via the semantic classifier). Writing such policies requires understanding the policy language and possibly writing code for custom conditions. It gives flexibility – e.g., you can allow an agent to use a tool only during business hours, or to limit API calls by parameters (if the PEP/PDP is extended to check those). However, this flexibility means more potential for error. It’s similar to writing firewall rules or IAM policies – powerful but sometimes complex to get right. [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)
*   *In the capability model*, the “policy” is embedded in which tokens you decide to issue to the agent and with what scope. For instance, rather than writing a rule that “Agent X can only access database Y read-only,” you would literally give the agent a DB connection token that only allows read queries on Y. There’s no separate policy language or engine – the token itself is the policy. This can simplify reasoning (especially for external parties: a token is easy to audit and share to show exactly what was permitted) and removes a layer of potential misinterpretation. But it also means the system must be designed so that *every action can be tied to a token issuance decision*. That might require enumerating more upfront or building an interactive process by which agents request tokens for new actions (which, as Ranjan Kumar’s blog notes, might involve an automated “capability service” validating the request). This could be a new paradigm for developers, whereas writing YAML policies is an extension of familiar concepts. [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents)

**Observability and Audit:** Both approaches deliver strong audit trails but in different formats.

*   **AGT** logs each action decision to its AuditLogger, which maintains a **hash-chained log** for tamper evidence. These logs include details like which agent DID took the action, what the action was, the outcome, etc. Additionally, because agents can be integrated with Entra ID, all their sign-ins or token exchanges can appear in Azure AD audit logs, providing a unified view alongside human users. AGT’s Agent SRE also provides out-of-the-box hooks to export metrics and traces (e.g., via OpenTelemetry) for agent actions/performance, making it easier for DevOps teams to monitor agent behavior with existing APM tools. Compliance mapping (like which agent actions correspond to which regulatory controls) is partly automated by Agent Compliance, giving organizations a head-start on audits. [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)
*   **Capability Model** produces an **audit log of capability events** – every time a token is issued, delegated, used, or revoked, an event is recorded (we called this the Capability Audit Graph). This log inherently contains the *chain of custody* (which token, issued by whom, allowed what) needed to answer who authorized an action. Because tokens are cryptographically signed, the log entries can be cross-verified for integrity (similar to how one would audit a blockchain or certificate transparency log). This model makes it straightforward to share audit evidence with external stakeholders: you can show verifiable tokens and signatures as proof of controls (aligning with regulatory needs for evidence of controls). What the capability model doesn’t inherently include is a higher-level analysis of those logs – that would be left to security analysts or separate SIEM rules. For example, AGT’s trust score provides a quick metric of an agent’s reliability, whereas in a pure capability system an analyst might have to manually examine the pattern of token uses or failures to judge an agent’s trustworthiness (though in practice one could build an analogous scoring on top of the logs). [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)

**Performance and Scalability:**

*   **Microsoft AGT** is optimized for speed and scale in software: intercepting a tool call and running it through the in-memory policy engine adds negligible latency (p99 < 0.1 ms) and minimal CPU overhead. It’s designed to handle thousands of checks per second per process, which generally far exceeds the throughput of LLM-driven agents (since the limiting factor is usually the LLM’s response time, e.g., hundreds of milliseconds). The AGT team consciously made the policy engine stateless and lightweight, akin to a network firewall or OS syscall filter, so that governance does not become a bottleneck. In scaled-out environments, you can run multiple agent instances each with its own policy engine, and manage them collectively (the SRE module can aggregate metrics). [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle), [\[digitalapplied.com\]](https://www.digitalapplied.com/blog/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-runtime-security) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)
*   **Capability Model** enforcement (token verification) is also fast and scalable – verifying a token signature and checking an action against an allow-list is on the order of microseconds to maybe a millisecond on modern hardware, quite comparable to AGT’s overhead. If implemented via a high-performance proxy (for instance, an Envoy filter or custom gRPC interceptor), it can likely approach similar sub-millisecond latency. One potential overhead is if the system uses remote calls for token issuance or verification on each action. To minimize latency, designs typically either (a) issue all needed tokens to the agent in advance (so runtime checks are purely local token validations) or (b) co-locate the capability service such that checking/issuing a token is near the agent (e.g., a sidecar container in the same pod). The **deterministic nature** of capability checks also means they can be highly optimized (e.g., a token might just be a structured, signed string that the gateway parses and verifies with no external calls). In short, a well-implemented capability framework *should* introduce similarly negligible latency, but it’s less turnkey at present – one would need to ensure the design is efficient (for example, avoid heavy database lookups on every action; instead use embedded token data). AGT has already done that optimization work for you.

**Reliability and Failure Modes:**

*   **AGT** includes extensive SRE features to ensure agent systems remain robust even when things go wrong: circuit breakers to cut off flaky or slow tools, automatic retries, error budgets to modulate agent behavior (e.g., temporarily pausing an agent that errors out too frequently), and chaos engineering utilities to test failure handling. These are beyond the scope of pure security but important for operations. AGT’s integrated SRE means adopting it helps not just with security but also with keeping agents stable in production (a key benefit if you’re dealing with unpredictable LLM behavior). [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)
*   **Capability Model** by itself doesn’t prescribe these reliability patterns, but it is compatible with them. One could certainly implement circuit breakers or rate limits as constraints within capability tokens (e.g., a token could include “max 5 uses per minute” and the gateway would enforce that). The difference is AGT provides many of these patterns out-of-the-box (and even ties them into monitoring/alerting), whereas with a DIY capability solution, the onus would be on your team to implement those safeguards or integrate separate tools.

## Strategic Considerations and Ecosystem Fit

Both solutions align in philosophy with **Zero Trust** and least privilege, but their strategic implications differ:

*   **Maturity and Ecosystem:** The Microsoft AGT is **a ready-made, open-source toolkit** with broad language support and a permissive MIT license, released in tandem with emerging enterprise needs and regulations. It’s backed by Microsoft’s involvement (with hints of moving to an open foundation) and has garnered community attention (e.g., on LinkedIn and in security circles) as a timely answer to AI governance needs. It directly integrates with Azure’s enterprise security stack (Entra ID, Purview, Defender) for a powerful combined solution, but is not limited to it (you can use AGT’s core without any Azure services if you handle identity and logs differently). This makes AGT appealing to enterprises already invested in Microsoft’s ecosystem, and also to others who want a quick solution with industry backing. However, as of April 2026 it is still **Public Preview** (not final GA) and subject to change. [\[digitalapplied.com\]](https://www.digitalapplied.com/blog/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-runtime-security) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle), [\[digitalapplied.com\]](https://www.digitalapplied.com/blog/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-runtime-security) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)

*   **Standardization and Vendor Neutrality:** The **Capability-Native approach** is aligned with a broader industry movement (influenced by decades of object-capability research and recent initiatives like the proposed Agent Permission Protocol by Crittora, and projects like *Tenuo* and *OpenTF’s trust tokens*) to create vendor-neutral standards for agent permissions. The emphasis is on using or extending open standards (W3C DIDs, Verifiable Credentials, OAuth/OIDC, etc.) to avoid lock-in and enable trust across platforms. For example, rather than building everything into Azure AD, a capability system could allow an AWS-hosted agent to securely interact with an Azure-hosted service by exchanging a signed token, without either side directly calling the other’s IdP. In theory, AGT also supports this via DIDs (and is itself open source), but in practice it’s a **Microsoft-initiated design** that naturally optimizes for Azure and Microsoft 365 scenarios (the synergy with Entra ID is a prime example). The capability model might have more appeal in heterogeneous environments or among organizations pushing for standards – in fact, Microsoft’s AGT team included a DID-based identity partly to ensure multi-cloud potential and has signaled interest in standardization efforts (like possibly donating to a foundation). [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/) [\[opensource...rosoft.com\]](https://opensource.microsoft.com/blog/2026/04/02/introducing-the-agent-governance-toolkit-open-source-runtime-security-for-ai-agents/)

*   **Feature Breadth vs. Focus:** AGT is a **full-stack solution for agent governance**. It doesn’t only handle permissioning; it also tackles reliability, plugin security, compliance mapping, etc. This is great for teams that want a one-stop solution, but it also means AGT might feel heavy if you’re only looking to solve one specific issue (like just preventing an agent from using certain APIs). The Capability-Native approach is **narrower in scope** – it’s primarily about the authorization and isolation piece. It doesn’t inherently fix your observability or compliance needs (though it facilitates them via logs), nor does it address things like agent retraining safety (AGT’s Agent Lightning) or plugin management (AGT’s Marketplace). If your primary concern is *just* least-privilege enforcement, the capability model is laser-focused on that; if you want a more all-encompassing “agent operations framework,” AGT has more out-of-the-box.

*   **Lock-In and Longevity:** AGT’s open-source nature and multi-language support reduce traditional vendor lock-in concerns, but it’s still a **Microsoft-led project with deep Azure tie-ins**. If your workflow is heavily on Azure (using Entra ID, Purview, etc.), AGT will integrate seamlessly and likely Microsoft will continue to invest in it as a pillar of their AI platform. If you operate in a multi-cloud environment or want a solution that doesn’t favor any vendor, the capability approach – being based on open protocols – may fit more naturally (and indeed, one could implement a capability system on top of AWS or GCP’s services using their primitives just as well). However, it’s worth noting Microsoft’s toolkit is also intended to be multi-cloud – it can run anywhere your agents run, and you can choose not to use any Azure-specific features. The difference is mainly whether you adopt Microsoft’s paradigm (with all its components, possibly including those you don’t need) or build a custom-fit capability system tailored to your exact needs (but then you own the integration effort).

To crystallize the comparison, the table below highlights key differences and similarities:

| **Characteristic**        | **Capability‑Native Proposal**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | **Microsoft AGT**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| ------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Auth model**            | Fine-grained **capability tokens** (explicit, one-token-per-authorized-action) [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents); external authorization service issues tokens; no ambient credentials                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | **Central policy engine** with rules (YAML/Rego/Cedar) in a PEP/PDP model [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle); decisions enforced in-process for each action                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **Default privilege**     | **Zero by default** – agent starts with no rights; must present tokens to do anything [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | Configurable allow/deny – typically deny by default unless policy grants an ability [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle), but agent process may still have baseline OS rights (mitigated by recommended containerization) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **Enforcement location**  | **Out-of-process** (sidecar gateway, OS kernel, or network proxy); forms a strong security boundary – agent cannot bypass without breaking sandbox [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle), [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents)                                                                                                                                    | **In-process** (middleware interceptors in agent runtime) – extremely fast and transparent to development, but not a foolproof isolation boundary on its own [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| **Delegation**            | **Cryptographic attenuation** – parent tokens yield child tokens with subset privileges; strictly enforced by token design (no expanding scope) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | **Scoped child identities** – parent agent spawns child with new DID/identity; child’s policy or trust level must be configured by developer to be ≤ parent’s (structure supports it but relies on correct policy) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| **Identity integration**  | **Decentralized first**, enterprise second – agents identified by DIDs (e.g., did:web or did:ion) with potential to be recognized across orgs [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/), [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents); enterprise identities (users, groups) feed into initial policy issuance (who gets which tokens) but are not needed to validate actions | **Enterprise first, also supports DIDs** – agents have DIDs (did:agentmesh) for internal trust and encryption [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle); can map to Azure AD identities for enterprise management (Agent ID), enabling use of directory features (Conditional Access, Access Reviews, M365 integration) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| **Policy expressiveness** | **Implicit in tokens** – encoded in what tokens are issued or refused. Simpler, binary decisions (allow/deny, with optional constraints like TTL or resource subset) [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents). Lacks high-level conditions unless implemented in issuance logic.                                                                                                                                                                    | **Rich rule language** – can use conditional logic, context (time, location, agent state), and even content classification (semantic analysis) in policies [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). More flexible (can adapt to scenarios without re-issuing credentials) but requires robust policy authoring and testing to avoid gaps.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| **Revocation & expiry**   | **Short-lived tokens** (minutes) by default, so authority auto-expires quickly [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). Immediate revocation by adding token IDs or agent IDs to a revocation list; all subsequent actions denied [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). Revocation does not require agent cooperation, since enforcement is outside agent.             | **Short-lived credentials** (AGT uses 15-min default for its internal tokens) [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/); integrated with Azure AD for disabling agent accounts which stops new token issuance [\[microsoft.github.io\]](https://microsoft.github.io/agent-governance-toolkit/tutorials/31-entra-agent-id-bridge/). AGT kill-switch can halt an agent mid-run (stop execution loop) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle); AGT also rotates credentials frequently. However, since enforcement is in-memory, revocation relies on the policy engine seeing a revoked status (e.g., via a push or the agent’s trust score hitting zero) – if the agent is completely compromised at the process level, revocation might be bypassed (again, why AGT should pair with infrastructure controls) [\[linkedin.com\]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/microsoft-agent-governance-toolkit-honest-take-venkat-peri-vwcle). |
| **Audit & forensics**     | **Cryptographically verifiable log** – each token and action can be logged and later validated against signatures, giving strong non-repudiation. Capability Audit Graph tracks “who permitted what” via token lineage [\[ranjankumar.in\]](https://ranjankumar.in/capability-tokens-fine-grained-authorization-for-non-deterministic-agents).
